method: AI-drafted from official standards text at the element level (Georgia lettered elements are individual standards); every pair requires human approval

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 1). Ohio: wind, temperature and precipitation can be used to document short-term weather changes that are observable. Georgia S1E1c: observe and measure current weather conditions with simple instruments and record temperature, precipitation and sky conditions.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 1). Ohio: yearly weather changes (seasons) are observable patterns in daily weather changes. Georgia S1E1d: analyze data to identify seasonal patterns of change such as temperature and rainfall/snowfall.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 1). Ohio: weather changes occur throughout the day and from day to day and can be described by wind, temperature and precipitation. Georgia S1E1a: represent data in tables/graphs to identify and describe different types of weather and their characteristics; overlapping content with a data-representation framing.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: the moon, sun and stars can be observed at different times of the day or night. Georgia SKE1a: classify objects according to those seen in the day sky, the night sky, and both.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: sun, moon and stars appear in different positions at different times of the day or night. Georgia SKE1b: model the changes that occur in the sky during the day, as day turns into night, during the night, and as night turns into day.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: the observable shape of the moon changes in size very slowly throughout the month. Georgia S2E2d: use personal observations to describe, illustrate, and predict how the appearance of the moon changes over time in a pattern; same content at a later grade with prediction added.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: objects can be sorted and described by the properties of the materials from which they are made. Georgia SKP1a: compare and sort objects made of different materials such as clay, cloth, plastic, wood, paper, and metal.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: objects and materials can be sorted and described by properties including color, size and texture. Georgia SKP1b: classify common objects according to their physical attributes (color, size, shape, weight, and texture).

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 1). Ohio: some objects and materials can be made to vibrate to produce sound; sound is produced when objects vibrate. Georgia S1P1d: construct an explanation supported by evidence that vibrating materials can make sound and that sound can make materials vibrate.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: living things have specific characteristics and traits, and they grow and reproduce. Georgia SKL1a: use observations to recognize the differences between organisms and nonliving objects; both hinge on what characterizes living things, but Georgia frames it as a living/nonliving distinction.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: living things have specific characteristics and traits. Georgia SKL1b: model how a set of organisms and nonliving objects are sorted into groups based on their attributes; overlaps on using characteristics of living things, with Georgia adding nonliving objects.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: living things grow and reproduce. Georgia SKL2c: observe similarities and differences of offspring to their parents and to other members of the same species; touches the reproduction idea but from the narrower offspring-resemblance angle.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: living things have physical traits, some of which are observable structures. Georgia SKL2a: argue from evidence how animals can be grouped according to their features; both center on observable physical traits, though Georgia is grouping-focused and animal-only.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: living things are made up of a variety of structures and some traits are observable. Georgia SKL2b: argue from evidence how plants can be grouped according to their features; the plant counterpart of the observable-traits idea.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 3). Ohio: some structures and behaviors of living things influence their survival. Georgia S3L1b: explain how external features and adaptations (camouflage, hibernation, migration, mimicry) allow animals to survive in their habitat; same traits-influence-survival idea at greater depth.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: time can be measured. Georgia SSKH3e: correctly use the time-measurement words day, week, month, year; both introduce units for measuring time.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: time can be measured. Georgia SSKH3c: correctly use today, tomorrow, yesterday; relative time vocabulary rather than units of measure, so the overlap is on marking time.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: heritage is reflected through diverse cultures and shown through arts, customs, traditions, family celebrations and language. Georgia SSKG1: describe the diversity of American culture by explaining the customs and celebrations of various families and communities.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: symbols and practices of the United States include the flag. Georgia SSKH2a: identify the national and state flags (United States and Georgia flags).

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: symbols and practices of the United States include the Pledge of Allegiance. Georgia SSKH2b: identify the Pledge of Allegiance as an American symbol/practice.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: symbols and practices of the United States include the National Anthem. Georgia SSKH2c: identify the Star Spangled Banner as the national anthem.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: terms of direction and distance, symbols and landmarks are used to talk about the relative location of familiar places. Georgia SSKG3: state the street address, city, state, and country where the student lives; both describe the location of familiar places, but Georgia targets absolute address rather than relative-location vocabulary.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: models and maps represent real places. Georgia SSKG2a: differentiate land and water features on simple maps and globes; applies the maps-represent-real-places idea to specific features.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: models and maps represent real places. Georgia SSKG2b: explain that maps and globes show a view from above; both establish how maps/globes represent the real world.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: models and maps represent real places. Georgia SSKG2c: explain that maps and globes show features in a smaller size; both address maps/globes as scaled representations of real places.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 3). Ohio: humans depend on and impact the physical environment in order to supply food, clothing and shelter. Georgia SS3H1b: compare how American Indians in each region used their environment to obtain food, clothing, and shelter; the same depend-on-environment concept in a historical context.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: humans depend on and impact the physical environment. Georgia SS2G2b: describe how historic figures and the Muscogee (Creek) and Cherokee adapted to and were influenced by their environments; human-environment interaction, but tied to specific Georgia figures.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: individuals share responsibilities and take action toward common goals in homes, schools and communities. Georgia SSKCG2: describe positive character traits of good citizens such as honesty, courtesy, respect and self-control; both address being a contributing community member, but Georgia frames it as traits rather than shared responsibility.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: the purpose of rules and authority figures is to provide order, security and safety. Georgia SSKCG1a: explain how rules are made and why.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: rules provide order, security and safety in the home, school and community. Georgia SSKCG1b: explain why rules should be followed.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: purpose of rules and authority figures is order, security and safety. Georgia SS2CG1: define the concept of government and the need for rules and laws; same purpose-of-rules idea extended to government.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: individuals have many wants and make decisions to satisfy those wants. Georgia SSKE4: explain that people must make choices because they cannot have everything they want.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: decisions to satisfy wants impact others. Georgia SS2E1: because of scarcity, people must make choices that result in opportunity costs; extends the choices-have-consequences idea with the opportunity-cost concept.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: goods are objects that satisfy wants and services are actions that satisfy wants. Georgia SSKE3a: distinguish goods from services.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 1). Ohio: goods are objects and services are actions that satisfy wants. Georgia SS1E1: identify goods that people make and services that people provide for each other; same goods/services distinction with a producer framing.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 3). Ohio: sunlight warms Earth's land, air and water, and amount of exposure affects warming or cooling. Georgia S3P1b: investigate with thermometers and chart the effect of sunlight on various objects — the same core idea that sunlight exposure warms materials.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 3). Ohio names the sun as the principal source of energy that warms Earth; Georgia S3P1a asks questions to identify sources of heat energy, explicitly including sunlight. Overlap is clear but Georgia frames it within a broader heat-energy standard.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: water changes from liquid to solid and solid to liquid due to changing energy. Georgia S2P1c: construct an explanation that some changes in matter caused by heating or cooling can be reversed, with heating or freezing of water as a named example.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: water on Earth is present in many forms and can change between liquid and solid. Georgia S1E1b: identify forms of precipitation (rain, snow, sleet, hail) as either solid (ice) or liquid (water) — touches solid vs. liquid water but only in a weather context, not the change process.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 5). Ohio: physical properties of water change between solid and liquid due to changing energy. Georgia S5P1b: argue from observations that physical changes in the state of water are due to temperature changes — same phenomenon, but Georgia adds a particle-motion depth beyond Ohio grade 1.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: properties of objects and materials change when exposed to conditions such as heating or cooling, and not all materials change the same way. Georgia S2P1c: explain that some changes in matter caused by heating or cooling are reversible and some are irreversible — the same core content.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio addresses properties of objects and materials and how they change; Georgia S2P1a has students describe and classify objects by physical properties (color, mass, texture, hardness). Overlaps on material properties but Georgia's element omits the change-under-conditions idea.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade K). Ohio: objects move in ways such as straight, zigzag, circular and back and forth, and pushes or pulls affect motion. Georgia SKP2a: investigate the relationship between an object's attributes and its motion (straight, circular, back and forth, fast and slow) when a force such as a push or pull is applied.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: a push or pull is a force that can make an object move faster, slower or change direction. Georgia S2P2a: plan and carry out an investigation to demonstrate how pushing and pulling on an object affects its motion — same core force-and-motion content.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio states that a force can make an object go faster, slower or in a different direction; Georgia S2P2b has students design a device to change the speed or direction of an object — same concept applied through an engineering-design framing.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: living things have basic needs met by obtaining materials, energy and water from the environment. Georgia S1L1b: compare and contrast the basic needs of plants (air, water, light, nutrients) and animals (air, water, food, shelter) — the same basic-needs core content.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 4). Ohio: plants get energy from sunlight and animals get energy from plants and other animals. Georgia S4L1b: model the flow of energy through a food web/food chain beginning with sunlight — the same energy-flow idea, but at food-web depth well beyond Ohio grade 1.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 3). Ohio: living things survive only in environments that meet their needs. Georgia S3L1c: use evidence to explain why some organisms can thrive in one habitat and not in another — the same survival-environment relationship, framed through Georgia habitats at a later grade.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: living things interact with their environments as they meet their needs, and resources must be available to meet those needs. Georgia S1L1c: design a solution to ensure that a plant or animal has all of its needs met — same needs-met-by-environment content with a design framing.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio notes that seasonal changes in the local environment directly impact the availability of resources. Georgia S1E1d: analyze data to identify seasonal patterns of change, including changes to the environment — overlaps on seasonal environmental change but not the resource-availability link.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade K). Ohio: time can be divided into categories such as past, present and future. Georgia SSKH3f: correctly use the chronology terms past, present, and future — the same time-category vocabulary.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade K). Ohio includes dividing time into categories such as months of the year. Georgia SSKH3e: correctly use the time words day, week, month, year — the same calendar-based time categories.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: the ways basic human needs are met have changed over time. Georgia SS1H1b: describe how everyday life of historical figures (food, clothing, homes, transportation, communication) is similar to and different from life today — same past-vs-present daily-needs comparison, framed through specific figures.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: maps can be used to locate and identify places. Georgia SS1G2: identify and locate the student's city, county, state, nation, and continent on a simple map or globe — the same map-use-to-locate-places skill.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade K). Ohio: maps can be used to locate and identify places. Georgia SSKG2a: differentiate land and water features on simple maps and globes — overlapping map-reading skill, but limited to land/water features rather than locating places.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: places are distinctive because of physical characteristics (landforms, bodies of water) and human characteristics (structures). Georgia SS1G3c: identify and describe landforms (mountains, deserts, valleys, coasts) — covers the physical-characteristics half but not human characteristics.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: families interact with the physical environment differently in different times and places. Georgia SS2G2b: describe how historic figures and the Muscogee (Creek) and Cherokee adapted to and were influenced by their environments — the same people-environment interaction across times and places, anchored to specific groups.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade K). Ohio: diverse cultural practices address basic human needs in various ways and may change over time. Georgia SSKG1: describe the diversity of American culture by explaining the customs and celebrations of various families and communities — same cultural-diversity core, without Ohio's change-over-time element.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: cultural practices address needs in various ways and may change over time. Georgia SS2H2a: compare and contrast Muscogee (Creek) and Cherokee cultures of the past to those of Georgians today — captures cultural practices changing over time, but only for two specific cultures.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade K). Ohio: individuals have responsibility to take action toward common goals in homes, schools and communities and are accountable. Georgia SSKCG2: describe positive character traits of good citizens such as honesty, respect, and self-control — overlapping civic-disposition content, but traits rather than responsibility for common goals.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade K). Ohio: rules exist in different settings and fairness should guide rules and consequences. Georgia SSKCG1a: explain how rules are made and why — same rules-and-their-purpose content, without Ohio's explicit fairness principle.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade K). Ohio addresses rules and the consequences for breaking them. Georgia SSKCG1b: explain why rules should be followed — same core content on the purpose of following rules.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: rules exist in different settings and should be guided by fairness. Georgia SS2CG1: define the concept of government and the need for rules and laws — clear overlap on why rules exist, framed at the government level.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: wants are unlimited and resources are limited, so individuals make choices. Georgia SS1E2: explain that scarcity is when unlimited wants are greater than limited resources — the same scarcity concept in nearly identical terms.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade K). Ohio: individuals make choices because they cannot have everything they want. Georgia SSKE4: explain that people must make choices because they cannot have everything they want — essentially the same statement.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: people produce and consume goods and services in the community. Georgia SS1E3: describe how people are both producers and consumers — the same production/consumption core content.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: people produce and consume goods and services. Georgia SS1E1: identify goods that people make and services that people provide for each other — the same goods-and-services content from the production side.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: people trade to obtain goods and services they want. Georgia SS2E3: explain that people usually use money to obtain goods and services and how money makes trade easier than barter — same trade-for-goods-and-services idea with added money/barter depth.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade K). Ohio: currency is used as a means of economic exchange. Georgia SSKE3b: identify that U.S. coins and dollar bills (paper money) are used as currency — the same currency-as-exchange concept.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: currency is used as a means of economic exchange. Georgia SS2E3: explain that people usually use money to obtain goods and services and that money makes trade easier than barter — same money-as-exchange concept with an added barter comparison.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 1). Ohio: wind results from energy transfer in the atmosphere and wind speed/direction can be measured. Georgia S1E1c: measure current weather conditions with simple instruments including a wind vane and record the data.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 4). Both concern measuring atmospheric conditions; Georgia S4E4a explains how weather instruments (including wind vane and anemometer) gather weather data, overlapping Ohio's measurement of wind but going beyond air/atmosphere basics.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 4). Ohio emphasizes investigating condensation and evaporation of atmospheric water vapor. Georgia S4E3b models the pathways of the water cycle including evaporation, condensation, and precipitation.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 4). Ohio relates cloud characteristics to weather rather than naming cloud types. Georgia S4E4c uses observations of cloud types and weather data to predict weather events.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 1). Ohio: short- and long-term weather changes affect temperature, precipitation, and wind. Georgia S1E1d analyzes data to identify seasonal patterns of change such as temperature and rainfall/snowfall.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 1). Ohio addresses changing weather including temperature and precipitation; Georgia S1E1a represents data to identify and describe different types of weather and their characteristics, a partial overlap with Ohio's focus on change over time.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: forces change an object's motion (increase, change direction, or stop). Georgia S2P2a investigates how pushing and pulling on an object affects its motion.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: the change in motion depends on the force applied and motion can change direction. Georgia S2P2b designs a device to change the speed or direction of an object.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 1). Ohio notes some forces act without touching, such as a magnet moving an object. Georgia S1P2b investigates how magnets attract and repel and their effect on common objects.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 4). Ohio: living things cause noticeable or slight changes in the environments where they live. Georgia S4L1c designs a scenario demonstrating the effect of a change on an ecosystem including living and non-living factors.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 3). Ohio: living things cause changes in their physical environments. Georgia S3L2a collects information on the sources and effects of pollution on plants and animals, a partial overlap focused on human-caused change.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 3). Ohio: organisms descend from ancestors, some now extinct and not represented today. Georgia S3E2a argues from fossils that they are evidence of past organisms and the environments in which they lived.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 7). Ohio: some past organisms are extinct and not all are represented by living organisms today. Georgia S7L5c analyzes fossil-record patterns documenting the existence, diversity, and extinction of organisms and their relationships to modern ones.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 1). Ohio: science and technology have changed daily life. Georgia SS1H1b describes how everyday life of historical figures differs from the present in areas like transportation and communication.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 4). Ohio: science and technology have changed daily life. Georgia SS4E1f gives examples of technological advancements (cotton gin, steamboat, locomotive) and their impact, a partial overlap centered on business productivity rather than daily life.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 1). Ohio: biographies show how people's actions have shaped the world. Georgia SS1H1a identifies the contributions made by historical figures such as Franklin and Jefferson.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 1). Ohio: maps and their symbols can be interpreted to answer questions about location. Georgia SS1G2 identifies and locates the student's city, county, state, nation, and continent on a simple map or globe.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: maps are interpreted to answer questions about location of places. Georgia SS2G2a identifies specific significant locations on a political or physical map.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 3). Ohio: the work people do is impacted by the human and physical characteristics of their place. Georgia SS3G3c explains how the physical geography of the colonies helped determine economic activities.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: work is shaped by the distinctive characteristics of the place where people live. Georgia SS2G2c describes how the region in which historic figures lived affected their lives.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 6). Ohio: human activities alter the physical environment, both positively and negatively. Georgia SS6G2b explains the environmental issue of the destruction of the rain forest in Brazil, an example of negative human alteration.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: cultures develop in unique ways influenced by the physical environment. Georgia SS2G2b describes how historic figures and the Muscogee and Cherokee adapted to and were influenced by their environments.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 3). Ohio: culture is shaped in part by the physical environment. Georgia SS3H1b compares how American Indians in each region used their environment to obtain food, clothing, and shelter.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 3). Ohio: respect for the rights of self and others includes responsible choices and accountability. Georgia SS3CG2a explains the necessity of respecting the rights of others and promoting the common good.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: making responsible choices and being accountable for personal actions. Georgia SS2CG3 gives examples of positive citizenship traits such as honesty, dependability, and trustworthiness.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: different rules and laws govern behavior in different settings. Georgia SS2CG1 defines the concept of government and the need for rules and laws.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade K). Ohio: there are different rules and laws that govern behavior. Georgia SSKCG1a explains how rules are made and why, a partial overlap on the nature and purpose of rules.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 1). Ohio: most people work in jobs producing specific goods and services. Georgia SS1E1 identifies goods that people make and services that people provide for each other.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade K). Ohio: most people work in jobs producing goods and services. Georgia SSKE1 describes the work that people do, such as police officer, farmer, doctor, and teacher.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: people use money to buy and sell goods and services. Georgia SS2E3 explains that people usually use money to obtain the goods and services they want and how money makes trade easier than barter.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade K). Ohio: people use money to buy and sell goods and services. Georgia SSKE3b identifies that U.S. coins and dollar bills are used as currency.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade K). Ohio: people earn income by working. Georgia SSKE2 explains that people earn income by working, a near-identical statement.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 1). Ohio: people earn income by working. Georgia SS1E4 explains that people earn income by working and must make choices about saving and spending, extending the same core idea.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: soil is composed of rock, organic material, water and air with measurable characteristics. Georgia S3E1b: plan and carry out investigations to describe properties (color, texture, capacity to retain water, plant-growth support) of soils and soil types (sand, clay, loam).

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: rocks have specific observable characteristics that allow them to be sorted and compared. Georgia S3E1a: ask questions and analyze data to classify rocks by physical attributes (color, texture, luster, hardness) using simple tests.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade K). Ohio: air and water are also nonliving Earth resources alongside soil and rock. Georgia SKE2a: ask questions to identify and describe earth materials—soil, rocks, water, and air—covering the same nonliving resources at an earlier grade.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 6). Ohio: renewable energy (wind, water, solar) can be replenished while nonrenewable energy (natural gas, coal, oil) is finite. Georgia S6E6a: determine the differences between renewable/sustainable energy resources (hydro, solar, wind) and nonrenewable resources (fossil fuels: oil, coal, natural gas).

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: reducing resource use, recycling and reusing can help conserve limited resources. Georgia S3L2b: research and communicate solutions such as conservation of resources and recycling of materials to protect plants and animals.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 6). Ohio: some Earth resources become limited due to overuse and contamination and can be conserved. Georgia S6E6b: design and evaluate solutions for sustaining the quality and supply of natural resources such as water, soil, and air.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: matter can change from one state to another by heating or cooling. Georgia S2P1c: provide evidence that some changes in matter caused by heating or cooling can be reversed and some are irreversible.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Same-grade partial overlap. Ohio names heat, electrical, light, sound and magnetic energy as forms of energy. Georgia S3P1a addresses only one of these—identifying sources of heat energy (sunlight, friction, burning)—so the overlap is real but incomplete.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 5). Ohio: individual organisms inherit many traits from their parents. Georgia S5L2b: ask questions to compare and contrast inherited and acquired physical traits.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade K). Ohio: offspring resemble their parents and each other. Georgia SKL2c: make observations to identify the similarities and differences of offspring to their parents and to other members of the same species.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 5). Ohio: some behavioral traits are learned through interactions with the environment and are not inherited. Georgia S5L2a: ask questions to compare and contrast instincts and learned behaviors.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: plants and animals have physical features and characteristics associated with their environments that influence survival. Georgia S3L1b: construct an explanation of how external features and adaptations allow animals to survive in their habitat.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 7). Ohio: inherited-trait differences give some individuals an advantage in surviving and reproducing. Georgia S7L5b: describe how genetic variation and environmental factors influence the probability of survival and reproduction of a species.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: the details of the life cycle differ for different organisms. Georgia S2L1d: develop models to illustrate the unique and diverse life cycles of organisms other than humans.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: organisms have life cycles through which they grow, reproduce, die and decay. Georgia S2L1a: ask questions to determine the sequence of the life cycle of common animals such as a mammal, bird, amphibian, and insect.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: daily life is influenced by the agriculture, industry and natural resources in different communities. Georgia SS3G3c: explain how the physical geography of the New England, Mid-Atlantic, and Southern colonies helped determine economic activities.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 8). Ohio: systems of transportation move people, products and ideas from place to place. Georgia SS8E1a: evaluate how the Interstate Highway System, airport, deepwater ports, and railroads interact to support the exchange of goods and services domestically and internationally.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade K). Ohio: communities may include diverse cultural groups. Georgia SSKG1: describe the diversity of American culture by explaining the customs and celebrations of various families and communities.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: members of local communities have rights and responsibilities. Georgia SS3CG2a: explain the necessity of respecting the rights of others and promoting the common good.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 5). Ohio: members of local communities have rights and responsibilities. Georgia SS5CG1a: explain the responsibilities of a citizen.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: individuals make the community a better place by taking action to solve problems and promote the common good. Georgia SS3CG2b: explain why it is important for citizens in a democratic society to participate in civic life (staying informed, voting, volunteering, communicating with public officials).

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: laws are rules that apply to all people, promote order and security, and protect rights. Georgia SS2CG1: define the concept of government and the need for rules and laws.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: governments have authority to make and enforce laws. Georgia SS3CG1c: state the main responsibility of each branch—executive (enforcing laws), legislative (making laws), judicial (determining if laws are fair).

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 8). Ohio: the structure of local governments may differ from one community to another. Georgia SS8CG6a: explain the origins and purposes of city, county, and special-purpose governments in Georgia.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 4). Ohio: both positive and negative incentives affect individuals' choices and behaviors. Georgia SS4E1b: explain how price incentives affect people's behavior and choices; GA limits this to price incentives, so the overlap is clear but narrower.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: individuals must make decisions because of scarcity of resources, and making a decision involves a trade-off. Georgia SS2E1: explain that because of scarcity, people must make choices that result in opportunity costs.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: making a decision involves a trade-off due to scarcity. Georgia SS3E4: explain the concept of opportunity cost as it relates to making a saving or spending choice.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: a consumer's wants are satisfied by using goods and services while a producer makes goods and provides services. Georgia SS3E3a: describe the interdependence of consumers and producers.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 1). Ohio distinguishes consumers from producers. Georgia SS1E3: describe how people are both producers and consumers.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: a market is where buyers and sellers exchange goods and services. Georgia SS3E3b: describe how goods and services are allocated by price in the marketplace.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: making decisions involves weighing costs and benefits. Georgia SS2E4: describe the costs and benefits of personal saving and spending choices.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 4). Ohio: a budget is a plan to help people make personal economic decisions and become more financially responsible. Georgia SS4E2: identify the elements of a personal budget (income, expenditures, saving) and explain why personal spending and saving decisions are important.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 6). Ohio: a budget is a plan to make personal economic decisions for the present and future. Georgia SS6E13b: understand that a budget is a tool to plan the spending and saving of income.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Ohio: the 13 colonies uniting around a common cause of liberty and justice. Georgia SS4H1a traces the events that shaped that revolutionary movement (Stamp Act, 'no taxation without representation,' Sons/Daughters of Liberty, Boston Massacre, Boston Tea Party).

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Ohio: colonies uniting to fight for independence during the American Revolution. Georgia SS4H1c covers the major events of that fight and the factors leading to American victory (Lexington and Concord, Saratoga, Yorktown).

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Ohio's 'common cause of liberty and justice... to form a new nation' is expressed in Georgia SS4H1d through the Declaration of Independence — why it was necessary and how it responded to tyranny. Matches the ideals half of Ohio's statement.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Both explicitly target the First Amendment. Ohio: the Constitution protects citizens' rights, five addressed in the First Amendment. Georgia SS4CG2: the importance of freedoms guaranteed by the First Amendment.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Ohio's 'system of limited government and protects citizens' rights' maps to SS4CG3b: the rights in the Bill of Rights and how it places limits on the powers of government. GA works through the full Bill of Rights rather than the First Amendment.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Direct three-branches match. Ohio: the Ohio and U.S. Constitutions separate the major responsibilities of government among three branches. Georgia SS4CG3a: how the three branches interact (checks and balances, separation of powers). GA lacks the state-constitution angle.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Ohio treats a constitution as a written plan for government; Georgia SS4H2b evaluates the issues debated while writing that plan at the Constitutional Convention (weaknesses of the Articles, states' rights). Related but historical rather than structural.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Ohio: the anti-slavery movement and the Underground Railroad. Georgia SS4H4a: contributions of Frederick Douglass, Sojourner Truth, and Harriet Tubman (with suffrage leaders Anthony and Stanton). Abolition content overlaps; GA is figure-focused and adds suffrage.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Both cover the War of 1812. Ohio: frontier conflicts with American Indians and Great Britain leading to the war. Georgia SS4H3a: its causes and events (burning of the Capitol, 'The Star-Spangled Banner'). Same war, different regional lens.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Ohio's Northwest Ordinance (process for territories to become states) sits inside Georgia SS4H3c's territorial-expansion story (Louisiana Purchase, Texas, Oregon, California). GA never names the Northwest Ordinance.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Ohio: interactions among American Indians, settlers, and immigrants producing cooperation, conflict and compromise. Georgia SS4H3b: the impact of westward expansion on American Indians (Trail of Tears, forced relocation). Overlap is settler–American Indian interaction; OH is broader.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Ohio: North, South and West regions developing from physical environments and economies. Georgia SS4H5b: states' rights and slavery increasing tensions between North and South. Both build sectional understanding; OH is geographic-economic, GA is a Civil War cause.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Georgia SS4E1c uses the differences between the economies of the North and South to teach specialization — the economic half of Ohio's regions standard.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Ohio is a map-skills standard (scale, cardinal/intermediate directions for relative location). Georgia SS4G1a is the content those skills serve: locating major physical features of the U.S. GA files map skills in a separate uncoded skills matrix.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Companion to SS4G1a: locating major man-made features (New York City, Boston, Washington D.C., the Erie Canal) matches the 'human characteristics' half of Ohio's map-skills statement.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Ohio: people modifying the environment with positive and negative consequences. Georgia SS4G2b: physical barriers that hindered and gateways that benefited territorial expansion. Both are human–environment interaction, but the causal direction is reversed.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 5). Ohio: U.S. economic development influenced by agriculture, industry and natural resources. Georgia SS5G2a: locating agricultural and industrial centers 1865–1900 and the factors (population, transportation, resources) that explain them.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 5). Same economic-geography content as SS5G2a, carried from the turn of the 20th century forward — matching Ohio's 'continues to influence and be influenced by' framing.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 5). Ohio: technological innovations originating in Ohio that benefited the U.S. (e.g., the Wright brothers of Dayton). Georgia SS5H1b: the impact of the Wright brothers, Carver, Bell, and Edison on American life.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Direct financial-literacy match. Ohio: saving a portion of income contributes to financial well-being; reducing spending to save more. Georgia SS4E2: elements of a personal budget (income, expenditures, saving) and why spending/saving decisions matter.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Ohio: entrepreneurs organize productive resources, take risks for profit, and compete. Georgia SS4E1f is the nearest element: technological advancements and their impact on business productivity. GA never frames entrepreneurship or risk directly.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Ohio: individuals' opportunities to act in and influence government. Georgia SS4CG1b grounds that in 'We the People' — consent of the governed / popular sovereignty — the principle underneath civic participation.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Georgia SS4CG1d (representative democracy/republic) covers the system through which Ohio's 'act in and influence their state and national government' happens. Concept-level overlap; GA omits rights-and-responsibilities language.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Ohio: laws can protect rights, provide benefits and assign responsibilities. Georgia SS4CG3b: the rights in the Bill of Rights, how it limits government, and citizens' responsibility to protect those rights. Both treat law as rights-protection.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 5). Ohio: identifying Earth's characteristic landforms. Georgia S5E1a: identifying surface features (deltas, sand dunes, etc.) caused by constructive/destructive processes. GA folds identification into process analysis; Ohio's 70%-water fact lands closer to S6E3a.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 5). Ohio: Earth's surface changes due to weathering (water, ice, wind, plant growth, catastrophic events). Georgia S5E1a: surface features caused by constructive and destructive processes — weathering is a named destructive process. Georgia has no grade-4 weathering element.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 6). Georgia S6E5d: identify types of weathering, agents of erosion and transportation, and environments of deposition — the middle-school revisit of Ohio's grade-4 weathering statement, at greater depth.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 5). Ohio: erosion and deposition — water, wind and ice remove and deposit rock, soil, sediment. Georgia S5E1a names the resulting features (deltas, sand dunes) formed by exactly those processes.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 5). Georgia S5E1b: interactive models collecting data on how surface-feature changes are caused by constructive/destructive processes — the modeling counterpart of Ohio's erosion/deposition statement.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 6). Georgia S6E5e: model how natural processes (weathering, erosion, deposition) and human activity change rocks and Earth's surface — Ohio's grade-4 content inside GA's grade-6 geology sequence.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 5). Ohio: total matter is conserved when objects break into pieces or dissolve. Georgia S5P1a investigates those same physical changes (manipulating, separating and mixing materials) but targets classifying the change, not conservation.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 5). Ohio: matter is conserved when a substance changes state. Georgia S5P1b: physical changes in the state of water due to temperature. Same phenomenon; GA doesn't make the conservation claim.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 8). Georgia's only explicit conservation-of-matter element is S8P1f (conservation of matter in a chemical reaction) — conceptually the true match for Ohio's conservation focus, but four grades later and at atomic-level depth.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 5). Ohio 4.PS.2 includes electrical energy transferring through circuits. Georgia S5P2b: design a complete, simple electric circuit and explain all necessary components.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 5). Ohio 4.PS.2 covers which materials conduct electrical energy. Georgia S5P2c: investigate common materials to determine if they are insulators or conductors of electricity.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 3). Ohio 4.PS.2 includes heat transferring from hot to cold objects with measurable temperature change. Georgia S3P1b: gather thermometer data illustrating the effects of heating — the investigative version, one grade earlier.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: changes in an organism's environment are sometimes beneficial and sometimes harmful; ecosystems change. Georgia S4L1c: design a scenario to demonstrate the effect of a change on an ecosystem, including living and non-living factors.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Georgia S4L1d: model changes to the flow of energy in an ecosystem when plants or animals become scarce, extinct, or over-abundant — a specific case of Ohio's environmental-change statement.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 7). Georgia S7L4c: how resource availability, disease, climate, and human activity affect organisms, populations, and ecosystems — the middle-school treatment of Ohio's grade-4 environmental-change idea.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 3). Ohio: fossils compared to one another and to present-day organisms as evidence of changing life and environments. Georgia S3E2a: construct an argument from fossil observations to show how they evidence past organisms and environments.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 4). Ohio: solar system includes sun and planets with unique distance, size, composition and movement. GA S4E1d: evaluate models of the solar system describing relative size, order, appearance, and composition of the planets and sun.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 6). Ohio: each planet has unique characteristics of distance from the sun, size, and composition. GA S6E1c: compare and contrast the planets in size relative to Earth, surface and atmospheric features, and relative distance from the sun.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 6). Ohio: comets, asteroids and meteoroids orbit the sun. GA S6E1e: compare and contrast the characteristics, composition, and location of comets, asteroids, and meteoroids.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 4). Ohio: the sun appears largest because it is the closest star, and some stars are larger or smaller than the sun. GA S4E1b: construct an argument on why some stars, including the sun, appear larger or brighter than others, limited to distance and size.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: the sun appears to be the largest star because it is closest to Earth. GA S2E1b: support the claim that although the sun appears brightest and largest, it is actually medium in size; overlaps but at earlier grade and less depth.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 4). Ohio: predictable cycles of Earth-sun motion, including revolution and rotation producing day and night. GA S4E2a: model why the length of day and night change throughout the year; overlaps on Earth-sun motion but frames it around day length.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 6). Ohio: cycles and patterns of motion between Earth and sun are predictable. GA S6E2c: relate the tilt of the Earth to the distribution of sunlight throughout the year; related Earth-sun motion but focused on seasons.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 4). Ohio: any change in speed or direction of an object requires a force. GA S4P3a: investigate the effects of balanced and unbalanced forces on an object; same force-and-motion idea at slightly lower depth.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 8). Ohio: speed is calculated from distance traveled in a period of time. GA S8P3a: identify patterns in the relationships between speed and distance; overlaps on speed/distance but is a middle-school analysis skill.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 8). Ohio: change in movement is based on the mass of the object and the amount of force. GA S8P3c: support the claim that the force needed to accelerate an object is proportional to its mass (inertia).

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 4). Ohio: light travels in a direction until it is reflected. GA S4P1b: describe the path light travels from a source to a mirror and how it is reflected.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 4). Ohio: light can be refracted when it moves from one medium to another. GA S4P1c: explore examples of when light is refracted using everyday materials such as prisms and a glass of water.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 4). Ohio: sound is produced by vibrating objects and the rate of vibration is related to pitch. GA S4P2a: produce sound and predict the effects of changing the strength or speed of vibrations.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 4). Ohio: organisms perform roles in an ecosystem and can be categorized as producers, consumers and decomposers. GA S4L1a: model the roles of producers, consumers, and decomposers in a community.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 4). Ohio: food webs identify relationships among producers, consumers and decomposers. GA S4L1b: illustrate the flow of energy through a food web/food chain including producers, consumers, and decomposers.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 4). Ohio: energy entering as sunlight is transferred by producers and passed from organism to organism in food webs. GA S4L1b: illustrate the flow of energy through a food web beginning with sunlight and including producers, consumers, and decomposers.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 7). Ohio: energy from sunlight is transformed by producers and passed among organisms. GA S7L4b: model the cycling of matter and the flow of energy among biotic and abiotic components of an ecosystem; same energy-flow concept at greater middle-school depth.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 3). Ohio: European exploration during the 1400s-1600s had lasting effects. GA SS3H2a: describe the reasons for and obstacles to the exploration of North America.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 3). Ohio: European exploration and colonization shaped the Western Hemisphere. GA SS3H2b: describe the accomplishments of explorers Cabot, Balboa, de Soto, Columbus, Hudson, and Cartier.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 6). Ohio: European colonization had lasting effects on the Western Hemisphere today. GA SS6H1b: describe the influence of the Spanish and Portuguese on the language and religions of Latin America; captures the lasting colonial effect.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 3). Ohio: latitude and longitude can be used to make observations about location. GA SS3G2: locate and describe the equator, prime meridian, and lines of latitude and longitude on a globe; GA does not extend to the climate generalizations Ohio adds.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 8). Ohio: regions can be determined using criteria including landform, climate, population, and economic characteristics. GA SS8G1b: distinguish among the five geographic regions of Georgia in terms of location, climate, agriculture, and economic contribution.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 2). Ohio: regions are determined using criteria such as landform and climate. GA SS2G1a: locate and compare the geographic regions of Georgia; introduces the region concept but without the multi-criteria analysis.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 6). Ohio: the variety of physical environments influences human activities. GA SS6G3a: explain how location, climate, and distribution of natural resources impact trade and affect where people live in Mexico, Brazil, and Cuba.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 6). Ohio: human activities modify the physical environment. GA SS6G2b: explain the environmental issue of destruction of the rain forest in Brazil; a clear case of humans modifying the environment.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 3). Ohio: American Indian tribes can be classified into cultural groups based on geographic similarities. GA SS3H1a: locate the regions where American Indians settled in North America (Arctic, Northwest, Southwest, Plains, Northeast, Southeast).

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 3). Ohio: American Indians developed unique cultures with different ways of life. GA SS3H1b: compare and contrast how American Indians in each region used their environment to obtain food, clothing, and shelter.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 6). Ohio: political, environmental, social and economic factors cause people to move in the Western Hemisphere. GA SS6H1d: explain the impact of poverty, the war on drugs, and migration to the United States on Latin America.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: factors cause people to move from place to place in the Western Hemisphere. GA SS5H1d: describe the reasons people immigrated to the United States, from where they emigrated, and where they settled.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 6). Ohio: the Western Hemisphere is culturally diverse in language and religion due to interactions of world cultures. GA SS6H1b: describe the influence of the Spanish and Portuguese on the language and religions of Latin America.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 6). Ohio: cultural diversity results from the interaction of a variety of world cultures. GA SS6H1a: describe the influence of African slavery on the development of the Americas; a major cultural influence on the hemisphere.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 6). Ohio: democracies, dictatorships and monarchies frame the relationship between those in power and citizens. GA SS6CG1a: explain citizen participation in autocratic and democratic governments (Mexico, Cuba, Brazil).

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 7). Ohio: monarchies are a category for understanding power and citizens. GA SS7CG3a: explain citizen participation in autocratic and democratic governments, including Saudi Arabia as an autocratic monarchy.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: the choices made by individuals and governments have present and future consequences. GA SS5E1a: describe opportunity costs and their relationship to decision-making across time.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 4). Ohio: choices have present and future consequences. GA SS4E1a: describe opportunity cost and its relationship to decision-making across time; same opportunity-cost concept.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 3). Ohio: availability of productive resources (entrepreneurship, human, capital, natural) promotes specialization. GA SS3E1d: entrepreneurship as combining natural, human, and capital resources to make a profit; names the same productive resources.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 6). Ohio: productive resources promote specialization that could lead to trade. GA SS6E2a: explain how specialization encourages trade between countries.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: productive resources and the division of labor impact productive capacity. GA SS5E1c: describe how specialization can improve standards of living and productivity, using Henry Ford's assembly line.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 4). Ohio: division of labor can affect productive capacity. GA SS4E1c: describe how specialization improves standards of living.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 6). Ohio: regions become interdependent when they specialize in what they produce best and trade. GA SS6E2a: explain how specialization encourages trade between countries.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 4). Ohio: specialization and trade increase the goods and services available. GA SS4E1e: describe how trade promotes economic activity.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 6). Ohio: workers improve their ability to earn income by gaining new knowledge and skills. GA SS6E3b: explain the relationship between investment in human capital (education and training) and GDP per capita.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: workers earn income and can improve their earning ability. GA SS5E3b: describe how people earn income by selling their labor to businesses; overlaps on labor and income though not on skill-building.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: minerals are naturally occurring inorganic solids with defined composition and measurable properties, identified by conducting tests. Georgia S6E5b: investigate the characteristics of minerals and how minerals contribute to rock composition.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: igneous, metamorphic and sedimentary rocks have unique characteristics used for identification/classification and to interpret formation history. Georgia S6E5c: classify rocks by their formation and explain how rocks change through geologic processes in the rock cycle.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio 6.ESS.2 also ties rock identification to a history of weathering (breakdown) and erosion (transport). Georgia S6E5d: identify types of weathering, agents of erosion and transportation, and environments of deposition.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: igneous, metamorphic and sedimentary rocks form in different ways (cooling magma, heat/pressure, weathering and lithification). Georgia S6E5c: explain how to classify rocks by their formation and how rocks change through the rock cycle.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: soil is unconsolidated material containing organic matter and weathered rock, forming in measurable horizons/layers. Georgia S6E5h: investigate evidence that soil is composed of layers of weathered rocks and decomposed organic material.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: rocks, minerals and soil are geologic resources with practical uses that are nonrenewable. Georgia S6E6a distinguishes renewable/sustainable from nonrenewable resources and how they are used; overlap is the renewable/nonrenewable framing, though GA emphasizes energy resources rather than geologic materials.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 8). Ohio: matter is made of atoms; molecules are two or more atoms joined chemically. Georgia S8P1e: analyze the periodic table to illustrate the structure and composition of atoms and simple molecules; GA develops this at greater depth.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 8). Ohio: elements are substances of a single kind of atom; molecules combine atoms. Georgia S8P1a: compare and contrast pure substances (elements and compounds) and mixtures; partial overlap on the element/compound concept.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 8). Ohio: changes of state explained by a particle model in motion; adding/removing energy causes temperature or phase change. Georgia S8P1b: model the movement of particles in solids, liquids, gases and plasma when thermal energy is added or removed.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 8). Ohio: heat is a process of energy transfer and temperature measures average particle motion. Georgia S8P2d: investigate the effects of heat transfer on molecular motion via conduction, radiation and convection; partial overlap on heat-as-transfer and particle motion.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 8). Ohio: two categories of energy, kinetic (from motion) and potential (from position). Georgia S8P2a: analyze data relating kinetic energy to mass and speed and potential energy to mass and height; same KE/PE distinction with GA adding quantitative analysis.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 8). Ohio: objects have kinetic energy from motion and potential energy from position. Georgia S8P2b: investigate the transformation between kinetic and potential energy within a system; partial overlap extending the KE/PE concept to transformations.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 8). Ohio: motion is described by speed and direction, with position and speed graphed as a function of time (no velocity/acceleration). Georgia S8P3a: analyze motion graphs for patterns in speed and distance; GA also introduces velocity and acceleration.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 5). Ohio: cells are the fundamental unit of life and all living things are composed of cells. Georgia S5L3a: gather evidence supporting a claim that plants and animals are comprised of cells too small to be seen without magnification.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 7). Ohio 6.LS.2 frames cell reproduction as transmitting genetic information from one generation to the next. Georgia S7L3a: explain the role of genes and chromosomes in inheriting a trait; partial overlap only on the genetic-information portion, not on cell division/growth and repair.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 7). Ohio: cells carry out specific functions with specialized parts (membrane, transport, energy, protein building, waste). Georgia S7L2a: explain how cell structures (nucleus, cytoplasm, membrane, wall, chloroplasts, lysosome, mitochondria) work together to obtain nutrients, grow, make materials and process waste.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 5). Ohio: cells are covered by a membrane and contain specialized parts. Georgia S5L3b: model and label parts of plant and animal cells (membrane, wall, cytoplasm, nucleus, chloroplasts); partial overlap on identifying cell components.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 7). Ohio: levels of organization within organisms include cells, tissues, organs, organ systems and whole organisms. Georgia S7L2b: use a conceptual model of how cells are organized into tissues, tissues into organs, organs into systems, and systems into organisms.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: regions can be classified and compared using landform, climate, population and economic characteristics. Georgia SS6G9a: compare how the location, climate and natural resources of Germany, the UK and Russia impact trade and where people live; GA applies the region-comparison skill to specific countries.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 7). Ohio: human activities modify the physical environment in the Eastern Hemisphere. Georgia SS7G2b: explain the relationship between poor soil and deforestation in Sub-Saharan Africa, an instance of humans altering the Eastern-Hemisphere environment.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 7). Ohio: the variety of physical environments in the Eastern Hemisphere influences human activities. Georgia SS7G3a: explain how the characteristics of the Sahara, Sahel, savanna and rain forest impact trade and affect where people live.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 7). Ohio: major world religions including Christianity, Islam and Judaism impacted the Eastern Hemisphere. Georgia SS7G8c: compare and contrast the prominent religions of Southwest Asia (Judaism, Islam and Christianity).

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 7). Ohio: major world religions including Buddhism and Hinduism, plus cultural diffusion, impacted the Eastern Hemisphere. Georgia SS7G12b: compare and contrast the belief systems originating in Southern and Eastern Asia (Buddhism, Hinduism, Shintoism and Confucianism).

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: governments range from monarchies to democracies and citizens' liberties vary with limits on governmental authority. Georgia SS6CG1a: explain citizen participation in autocratic versus democratic governments, reflecting how citizen roles differ by government type.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio categorizes governments including different forms of democracy. Georgia SS6CG1b: describe the two predominant forms of democratic government, parliamentary and presidential; partial overlap on classifying government forms.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: the fundamental questions of economics are what to produce, how to produce and for whom to produce. Georgia SS6E1a: compare how traditional, command and market economies answer what, how and for whom to produce.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: when regions/countries specialize, global trade occurs. Georgia SS6E2a: explain how specialization encourages trade between countries.

alignments.subject: social_studies
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: when selecting items to buy, individuals weigh costs and benefits and compare price and quality. Georgia SS6E13b: understand that a budget is a tool to plan spending and saving of income; partial overlap on personal spending decisions rather than cost/benefit comparison specifically.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 6). Ohio: the hydrologic cycle moves water through Earth's spheres with thermal-energy transfer and is central to weather. Georgia S6E3b investigates the sun's energy driving atmospheric conditions that cycle water (evaporation, condensation, precipitation, infiltration, groundwater, runoff).

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 4). Ohio: thermal energy is transferred as water changes state throughout the water cycle. Georgia S4E3a observes the flow of energy in water as it changes states between solid, liquid, and gas, matching the energy/state-change portion at a lower grade.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 6). Ohio: solar thermal-energy transfers plus Earth's rotation drive atmospheric currents (jet stream) and global climate. Georgia S6E4c models how unequal heating and Earth's rotation cause local and global wind systems.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 6). Ohio: thermal-energy transfers in the ocean form currents (Gulf Stream) shaped by density and salinity. Georgia S6E3d analyzes the causes and effects of waves, currents, and tides in Earth's systems.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 6). Ohio: the atmosphere has defined layers with distinct properties and a mixture of gases including CO2 and other trace gases. Georgia S6E4a compares and contrasts the composition of Earth's atmospheric layers and greenhouse gases.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 6). Ohio: the moon's changing position relative to Earth and sun produces the phases of the moon. Georgia S6E2a models the phases of the moon by showing the relative positions of the sun, Earth, and moon.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 6). Ohio: relative positions of Earth, moon, and sun cause solar and lunar eclipses. Georgia S6E2b constructs an explanation of the cause of solar and lunar eclipses.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 6). Ohio: gravitational force between Earth and moon causes daily tides, including spring and neap tides. Georgia S6E3d analyzes the causes and effects of tides (with waves and currents) in Earth's systems.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 6). Ohio: Earth's 23.5-degree axial tilt and revolution control the direct sunlight received, causing seasons. Georgia S6E2c relates the tilt of the Earth to the distribution of sunlight throughout the year and its effect on seasons.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 4). Ohio: the tilt of Earth's axis plus its revolution around the sun cause seasons. Georgia S4E2c explains how Earth's orbit with its consistent tilt affects seasonal changes, at an earlier grade.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 8). Ohio: the conceptual introduction to the Periodic Table, organizing elements by observable properties and as metals/non-metals/metalloids. Georgia S8P1e analyzes patterns within the periodic table but emphasizes atomic structure, a deeper treatment than Ohio's observable-property focus.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 8). Ohio: elements are classified by similar properties such as density, melting point, and boiling point. Georgia S8P1c compares and contrasts physical properties of matter including density, melting point, and boiling point.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 8). Ohio: matter can change but the number and types of atoms and total mass remain constant in a closed system. Georgia S8P1f explains conservation of matter in a chemical reaction, including differences between products and reactants.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 8). Ohio: physically combining substances forms a mixture that can be separated through physical processes. Georgia S8P1a compares and contrasts pure substances and mixtures, including heterogeneous and homogeneous mixtures.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 8). Ohio: energy can be transformed from one form to another while the total amount remains the same. Georgia S8P2c constructs an argument about the type of energy transformations within a system; overlap in transformation with less explicit conservation framing.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 8). Ohio: energy is transformed or transferred but never lost. Georgia S8P2b investigates the transformation between kinetic and potential energy within a system, a concrete case of energy conservation.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 8). Ohio: thermal energy can be transferred through radiation, convection, and conduction. Georgia S8P2d investigates the effects of heat transfer via conduction, radiation, and convection.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 8). Ohio: mechanical and electromagnetic waves transfer energy when they interact with matter. Georgia S8P4a develops explanations about the similarities and differences between electromagnetic and mechanical waves.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Cross-grade (GA grade 5). Ohio: an electrical circuit transfers energy from a source to a device. Georgia S5P2b designs a complete simple electric circuit and explains its necessary components, covering the circuit portion at an earlier grade.

alignments.subject: science
alignments.rationale: Same-grade match. Ohio: energy flows and matter is transferred continuously among organisms and their environment, with total matter and energy remaining constant.
